title: How to See the Giant Asteroid That Will Pass by Earth This Weekend
gist: ESA confirmed asteroid (152637) 1997 NC1 (estimated 700m–1.6km diameter) made its closest approach to Earth in 400 years on June 27, 2026, passing 2.56 million km away (6.6 lunar distances) with no impact risk; it reached magnitude 10 requiring at minimum a 100mm telescope to observe, and will not return this close until 2133.

insights.claim: Asteroid (152637) 1997 NC1, estimated between 700 meters and 1.6 kilometers in diameter, made its closest Earth approach in 400 years on June 27, 2026 at 2.56 million kilometers (6.6 lunar distances) with no impact risk; it will not come this close again until 2133.
insights.speaker: Jorge Garay
insights.so_what: The 400-year return interval underscores the rarity and scientific value of long-arc asteroid tracking programs; no imminent planetary defense concern.
insights.locator: The closest approach will occur on Saturday, June 27

insights.claim: At closest approach, 1997 NC1 reached approximately magnitude 10—40 times fainter than the limit of naked-eye visibility—requiring a minimum 100mm aperture commercial telescope or 15x70 astronomical binoculars to observe, with 150–200mm providing a more comfortable view.
insights.speaker: Jorge Garay
insights.so_what: High-profile asteroid events drive consumer telescope demand; near-full moon conditions reduced viewing quality and likely drove amateur astronomers toward livestream alternatives.
insights.locator: it will reach a brightness close to magnitude 10

insights.claim: The Virtual Telescope Project hosted livestreams of the 1997 NC1 flyby on June 26 and 27, providing public access to real-time asteroid tracking without requiring personal equipment—demonstrating growing infrastructure for public planetary defense awareness.
insights.speaker: Jorge Garay
insights.so_what: Online astronomy platforms are building public audiences around planetary defense events; a growing channel for science communication and space-sector public engagement.
insights.locator: the Virtual Telescope Project is hosting livestreams
